current_fact.fact_text: In 1965, NASA launched Gemini 3, the United States' first two‑person spaceflight, carrying astronauts Gus Grissom and John Young. During the mission, Young famously smuggled a corned beef sandwich onboard, briefly filling the capsule with floating crumbs and giving this pioneering test of orbital maneuvering an oddly human, humorous twist.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

current_fact.match_reason: A literal spaceflight with playful human moments pairs naturally with an episode about reimagining empty space as creative potential and forward motion.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2001, the Russian space station Mir was deliberately deorbited after 15 years in orbit, breaking apart in Earth’s atmosphere and falling into the Pacific Ocean near Fiji. Mir had hosted more than a hundred astronauts and cosmonauts, becoming a floating laboratory and home that quietly defined an entire era of human life in space.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The gentle, reflective mood of ‘Slow Planet’ mirrors the controlled, almost meditative farewell to a long‑lived space station whose orbit was gradually slowed into a final descent.

queue.fact_text: In 1806, after reaching the Pacific Ocean, Lewis and Clark’s Corps of Discovery turned back east to begin their grueling journey home through unmapped terrain. Their return trek was as perilous and important as the outward voyage, transforming scattered observations into a mental and cartographic map that would reshape how Americans imagined the continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A long, uncertain voyage across rivers and wilderness resonates with a sea‑journey episode about shared resilience, travel companionship, and navigating together.

queue.fact_text: On March 23, 1977, David Frost began his series of in‑depth television interviews with former U.S. President Richard Nixon, confronting him about Watergate and the secret tapes. The conversations, part journalism and part public reckoning, revealed how power, truth, and image can collide in front of a camera.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: Frost–Nixon is a rare moment of televised accountability around abuse of power, echoing an episode centered on political rage, protest, and challenging authority in public.

queue.fact_text: In 1839, a powerful earthquake shattered the former imperial capital of Inwa (Ava) in present‑day Myanmar, toppling royal structures and helping end its long run as a political center. The quake was so destructive that the capital was eventually relocated, showing how a single geological event can redraw the map of human power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A devastating natural disaster that forces a capital to move fits with an episode about weathering storms and finding hope and continuity through upheaval.

queue.fact_text: The Battle of Topáter in 1879 was the opening clash of the War of the Pacific, where a small Bolivian force tried to hold a desert river crossing against Chilean troops. Fought in the stark Atacama region, it turned an almost unimaginably dry landscape into the backdrop for a struggle over nitrate‑rich coastal territory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A desert battle over resources aligns with an episode rooted in desert cultures, resilience, and the soundscape of sand, survival, and contested homelands.

queue.fact_text: In 1994, a USAF F‑16 collided with a C‑130 near Pope Air Force Base in North Carolina, sending flaming debris onto a crowded staging area and killing 24 soldiers on the ground. The tragedy, later called the Green Ramp disaster, led to sweeping changes in safety procedures for complex air operations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The sudden, catastrophic collision and fiery impact echo the raw, high‑voltage intensity suggested by an episode called ‘Electric Shock,’ pairing dramatic energy with reflective listening.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1978, the first UNIFIL peacekeeping troops arrived in Lebanon to patrol a tense border zone known as the Blue Line. Their presence turned a narrow strip of land into a fragile laboratory for coexistence, where multinational forces walk a daily line between ceasefire and renewed conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: Peacekeeping along a contested border requires constant, mindful balance; an episode about movement as meditation and perspective‑shifting travel complements this delicate mission.

queue.fact_text: In 1909, just weeks after leaving the White House, Theodore Roosevelt boarded a ship from New York for a year‑long scientific safari in Africa sponsored by the Smithsonian and National Geographic. He and his team collected thousands of specimens, turning a former president into a field naturalist whose adventures filled museums and magazines back home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Roosevelt’s safari was part science, part obsession with wildlife, much like an episode built around a personal quest to meet orcas and understand nature through immersive encounters.

queue.fact_text: On March 23, 2014, the World Health Organization announced Ebola cases in rural southeastern Guinea, quietly marking the beginning of the largest Ebola outbreak in history. What started in forested villages would soon test global health systems, highlight frontline courage, and expose how viruses exploit the smallest gaps in our connected world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A global health crisis forced scientists and societies into a steep learning curve about viruses, systems, and resilience, fitting an episode that delves into science, adaptation, and the emotional side of learning.

queue.fact_text: In 1991, the Revolutionary United Front, backed by Charles Taylor’s forces from Liberia, crossed into Sierra Leone to overthrow the government, sparking an 11‑year civil war notorious for child soldiers and conflict diamonds. The invasion turned remote border regions into the first pages of a brutal chapter in West African history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: While the fact is about West African conflict, the Afrobeat episode amplifies another side of the region: political music, resistance, and joy, offering cultural context and a counter‑narrative to violence.

queue.fact_text: On March 23, 2020, UK Prime Minister Boris Johnson announced the country’s first nationwide COVID‑19 lockdown, telling millions to stay at home almost overnight. Streets fell silent, birdsong grew louder in cities, and living rooms became offices, gyms, and concert halls in a massive social experiment in slowing a virus.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Lockdown radically intensified our relationship with screens and phones; an episode about digital dependence and disconnection resonates with that sudden shift to life mediated through devices.

queue.fact_text: In 1983, U.S. President Ronald Reagan unveiled the Strategic Defense Initiative, a futuristic plan to use space‑based and ground‑based systems to intercept nuclear missiles. Nicknamed “Star Wars,” it blended physics, lasers, and geopolitics into a vision that felt as much like science fiction as strategy.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A grand, space‑tinged defense concept with a sci‑fi nickname pairs with a cosmic, exploratory episode that feels like plotting a different kind of destination beyond Earth.

queue.fact_text: In March 1913, a devastating tornado outbreak swept through the central United States while catastrophic flooding ravaged the Ohio River watershed, killing hundreds. The overlapping disasters became an early case study in how extreme weather events can cascade, overwhelming communities and infrastructure at once.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: The story of overwhelming natural forces and rebuilding speaks to an episode about disciplined physical practice and resilience, where bodies move like weather through heat, rain, and repetition.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2018, Peru’s president Pedro Pablo Kuczynski resigned amid a sweeping corruption scandal tied to Operation Car Wash, a Brazilian investigation that rippled across Latin America. The downfall showed how financial trails and plea deals can unravel political power far from their original source.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A Latin American corruption scandal and forced resignation align with an episode about a politically outspoken Latin artist who dissects power, inequality, and accountability through music.

queue.fact_text: In 2009, FedEx Express Flight 80, a McDonnell Douglas MD‑11 cargo jet, crashed during landing at Tokyo’s Narita Airport after bouncing and rolling over in high winds, killing both pilots. The accident highlighted how even routine cargo flights demand delicate control at the edge of physics and weather.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A high‑risk landing at the end of a long route connects to an episode themed around motion, travel, and the precarious but exhilarating feel of being ‘on the go.’

queue.fact_text: On March 23, 2003, the Battle of Nasiriyah became one of the first major engagements of the Iraq invasion, where U.S. forces fought through an urban maze of bridges, ambushes, and civilian areas. The chaos of miscommunication and resistance there foreshadowed a conflict far more complex than initial plans imagined.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A city turned into a battlefield contrasts starkly with the idea of the dancefloor as a safer public space; pairing them underlines how contested urban space can be reclaimed through bodies, rhythm, and community.

queue.fact_text: In 1982, a military coup in Guatemala ousted President Fernando Romeo Lucas García and brought General Efraín Ríos Montt to power, ushering in one of the most violent phases of the country’s civil war. Ríos Montt’s regime would later be associated with scorched‑earth campaigns against Indigenous communities and historic genocide trials.

episode.title: Morning Drops #26 - Canserbero
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the story of Canserbero, the Venezuelan rapper who reshaped Latin American hip hop with his raw voice and deeply reflective lyrics. A journey into his music, his vision, and the legacy of an artist who transformed personal and social struggles into powerful storytelling.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como una película y su tragedia, comedia y ficción

queue.match_reason: A brutal Latin American dictatorship and civil war pair with an episode exploring a Latin rapper whose conscious lyrics confront violence, injustice, and the weight of political realities.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1980, Archbishop Óscar Romero of El Salvador delivered a sermon urging soldiers to disobey orders to kill civilians, telling them, “In the name of God, stop the repression.” He was assassinated the very next day while saying Mass, and decades later was canonized as a saint for his defense of human rights.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: Romero’s moral courage and prophetic voice align with an episode celebrating powerful voices that comfort, confront, and demand justice in soulful, emotionally charged ways.

queue.fact_text: In 1918, during the German Spring Offensive of World War I, the 10th Battalion of the Royal West Kent Regiment was virtually annihilated, with many of its soldiers taken prisoner. The battalion’s disappearance in the chaos of trenches and artillery became a haunting example of how quickly entire units could vanish on the Western Front.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A battalion erased in a single offensive invites sober remembrance and reflection, well matched with an episode about looking back, healing, and living with the aftermath of sudden trauma.

queue.fact_text: The Russian Mir space station’s 2001 controlled deorbit didn’t just end a spacecraft; it created a brief, unplanned light show for people watching from Pacific islands and ships below. Fragmented modules, glowing from re‑entry, streaked across the sky like a man‑made meteor shower before disappearing into the ocean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: The image of a burning, falling space station streaking like cosmic fireworks fits perfectly with a bright, machine‑driven ‘Cosmic Monday’ dedicated to futuristic dance sounds.

queue.fact_text: During Roosevelt’s 1909–10 African expedition, the team didn’t just hunt big game; they preserved meticulous field notes, photographs, and specimens that scientists still reference. The safari blurred the line between colonial spectacle and early conservation science, forcing later generations to reconsider how knowledge and power traveled together.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: This secondary angle on Roosevelt’s expedition emphasizes observation and science in wild places, echoing the reflective, nature‑focused journey of following orcas through cold seas.

queue.fact_text: The 2014 Ebola outbreak, first signaled in rural Guinea, also accelerated the development of experimental vaccines and treatments that had languished in labs. In less than a decade, what began as a terrifying unknown in forest villages helped push global science toward faster responses to emerging diseases.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The way a virus crosses borders and reshapes global cooperation parallels how trip‑hop blurred musical boundaries, with a moody, cinematic tone fitting the gravity and complexity of an outbreak story.

queue.fact_text: When Boris Johnson announced the UK’s first COVID lockdown in 2020, air traffic plummeted so sharply that seismologists could detect a global drop in human‑made ground vibrations. The pandemic briefly turned our noisy, vibrating planet into a quieter instrument, making the Earth’s own subtle signals easier to hear.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A suddenly quieter Earth that lets us hear hidden vibrations reflects the episode’s invitation to go below the surface, slow down, and immerse in deeper layers of sound and sensation.

published.fact_text: In 1965, NASA launched Gemini 3, the United States' first two‑person spaceflight, carrying astronauts Gus Grissom and John Young. During the mission, Young famously smuggled a corned beef sandwich onboard, briefly filling the capsule with floating crumbs and giving this pioneering test of orbital maneuvering an oddly human, humorous twist.
